notes: Chef review wave 11. Backstage source notes only; public pages should remain recipe-first.

recipes.review_summary: Basbousa was broadly correct. This pass makes the semolina rest and syrup soak honest, keeps yoghurt, butter, almonds, rose water, and removes halal as a blanket tag.
recommended_changes: Rest the semolina batter before baking.
Pour cool syrup over hot cake.
Let the cake soak before serving.
recipes.official_source_status: Sudanese and regional sources consistently describe basbousa as a semolina cake soaked in sugar syrup, often with yoghurt, butter, coconut, almonds, rose water, or orange blossom.

recipe.description: Sudanese-style basbousa, a syrup-soaked semolina cake with yoghurt, butter, almonds, coconut, and rose water.
recipe.story: The syrup has to meet the cake at the right moment. Cold syrup over hot basbousa gives a tender, sweet crumb without making it soggy.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, coffee, fresh fruit, or extra syrup

recipes.review_summary: Sudanese ful was good but the page ignored overnight soaking. This pass makes the prep time honest and keeps sesame oil, lemon, cumin, onion, garlic, and chile.
recommended_changes: Soak dried fava beans overnight or use cooked beans.
Cook until very soft and mash roughly.
Finish Sudanese-style with sesame oil and lemon.
recipes.official_source_status: Sudanese references consistently describe ful as slow-cooked fava beans served with sesame oil, lemon, cumin, onion, garlic, chile, and bread.

recipe.description: Sudanese ful medames, slow-cooked fava beans mashed with cumin, lemon, garlic, onion, chile, and sesame oil.
recipe.story: Ful should be creamy but never lifeless. Keep a little texture in the beans and let the sesame oil finish the bowl.
recipe.best_served_with: Warm bread, boiled eggs, salad, feta, pickles, or extra lemon

recipes.review_summary: Kisra with mullah tagalia needed fermentation time and a more accurate tagalia description. This pass centers onions, tomato paste, minced or dried beef, oil, coriander, and red pepper.
recommended_changes: Ferment kisra batter for 12 to 24 hours.
Cook tagalia until onions darken and oil rises.
Remove halal and add gluten-free because the batter is sorghum-led.
recipes.official_source_status: Sudanese references describe kisra as fermented sorghum flatbread and mullah tagalia as onion, tomato paste, minced or dried beef, oil, and spices cooked thick.

recipe.description: Sudanese kisra with mullah tagalia, fermented sorghum crepes served with thick onion, tomato, meat, coriander, and red pepper sauce.
recipe.story: Good kisra brings the tang, and tagalia brings the depth. Cook the onions long enough for the sauce to turn dark and glossy.
recipe.best_served_with: Salad, lime, extra chile, or asida

recipes.review_summary: Chipsi mayai was accurate. This pass removes halal as irrelevant, keeps the fry-and-omelette method, and names kachumbari/pili pili as the table.
recommended_changes: Fry chips first.
Pour seasoned eggs over chips in the pan.
Serve with kachumbari, ketchup, or pili pili.
recipes.official_source_status: Tanzanian references consistently describe chipsi mayai as a street-food omelette made with French fries and eggs.

recipe.description: Tanzanian chipsi mayai, a street-food omelette built around crisp fries, eggs, onion, tomato, and chile.
recipe.story: The chips have to stay proud inside the egg. Fry them first, then set the omelette around them instead of drowning them.
recipe.best_served_with: Kachumbari, ketchup, pili pili sauce, or extra tomato salad

recipes.review_summary: Tanzanian pilau was strong. This pass removes halal guarantee, keeps whole spice depth, and adds ginger as a common coastal note.
recommended_changes: Caramelize onions well.
Use whole spices with garlic and ginger.
Cook rice in meat or vegetable stock until dry and fluffy.
recipes.official_source_status: Tanzanian and Zanzibari sources consistently describe pilau as spiced rice with onions, whole spices, meat or potatoes, garlic, ginger, and stock.

recipe.description: Tanzanian pilau, Zanzibar-influenced spiced rice with caramelized onions, meat, potatoes, garlic, ginger, and whole spices.
recipe.story: Pilau lives in the onion and spice oil. Let the onions color deeply before rice and stock go in.
recipe.best_served_with: Kachumbari, banana, pili pili, coconut beans, or grilled meat

recipes.review_summary: Ugali na nyama was fine structurally. This pass removes halal guarantee and makes beef stew timing a bit more realistic.
recommended_changes: Cook beef stew until tender before making ugali.
Use maize flour and water for a firm ugali.
Serve immediately while both are hot.
recipes.official_source_status: Tanzanian references consistently describe ugali as a maize staple eaten with meat, fish, beans, or vegetable stews.

recipe.description: Tanzanian ugali na nyama, firm maize ugali served with slow-simmered beef, tomato, onion, garlic, and pepper stew.
recipe.story: Start the stew first. Ugali takes minutes, but nyama needs time to soften and make sauce worth scooping.
recipe.best_served_with: Kachumbari, sukuma wiki, pili pili, or avocado

recipes.review_summary: Akoumé was accurate. This pass keeps corn dough, slurry-first cooking, and pairing with sauces, while tightening naming and story.
recommended_changes: Use corn flour or a corn-flour blend.
Start with slurry to prevent lumps.
Stir until thick enough to mound.
recipes.official_source_status: Togolese references consistently describe akoumé as a firm corn-flour dough served with sauces such as gboma dessi.

recipe.description: Togolese akoumé, a firm corn-flour dough shaped into a smooth mound and served with rich sauces.
recipe.story: The trick is smoothness. Start with a slurry, then add flour gradually and stir until the dough holds its shape.
recipe.best_served_with: Gboma dessi, tomato sauce, okra sauce, grilled fish, or chicken stew

recipes.review_summary: Gboma dessi was too seafood-specific for a dish that commonly uses meat, shrimp, crab, or smoked fish. This pass adds garlic, ginger, chile, tomato sauce, and longer stew time.
recommended_changes: Use spinach or gboma greens with tomato, onion, garlic, ginger, palm oil, and chile.
Allow beef, crab, shrimp, or smoked fish as common proteins.
Simmer longer so greens and sauce integrate.
recipes.official_source_status: Togolese references describe gboma dessi as a leafy tomato sauce served with akoumé or fufu and commonly cooked with meat, shrimp, crab, or smoked fish.

recipe.description: Togolese fufu with gboma dessi, pounded yam served with spinach tomato stew, palm oil, garlic, ginger, chile, and seafood or meat.
recipe.story: Gboma dessi needs the greens to cook into the tomato base. Let the sauce thicken until it clings to the fufu.
recipe.best_served_with: Akoumé, ablo, rice, or extra chile sauce

replace_ingredients.name: beef, smoked fish, crab, or shrimp
red palm oil or vegetable oil

recipes.review_summary: Koklo meme was close, but prep time ignored the marinade. This pass increases prep time and removes halal guarantee.
recommended_changes: Marinate chicken with ginger, garlic, chile, citrus, oil, salt, and pepper.
Grill over charcoal.
Rest before cutting.
recipes.official_source_status: Togolese references describe koklo meme as grilled chicken marinated with ginger, garlic, chile, citrus, and spices.

recipe.description: Togolese koklo meme, charcoal-grilled chicken marinated with ginger, garlic, chile, lime, oil, salt, and pepper.
recipe.story: The marinade should get under the skin and into the cuts. Let the chicken sit, then grill until the edges char.
recipe.best_served_with: Akoumé, rice, salad, piment sauce, or fried plantains

recipes.review_summary: Brik was accurate. This pass removes halal guarantee and keeps malsouka, egg, tuna, capers, parsley, onion, lemon, and harissa.
recommended_changes: Use malsouka or brik pastry.
Keep the egg yolk soft if possible.
Serve immediately with lemon and harissa.
recipes.official_source_status: Tunisian sources consistently describe brik à l'oeuf as thin pastry filled with egg, tuna, capers, parsley, and onion, then fried crisp.

recipe.description: Tunisian brik à l'oeuf, a crisp malsouka pastry filled with egg, tuna, capers, parsley, onion, lemon, and harissa.
recipe.story: Brik waits for no one. Assemble, fry, drain briefly, and eat while the shell still shatters.
recipe.best_served_with: Lemon wedges, harissa, salad mechouia, or olives

recipes.review_summary: Lablabi was accurate but ignored overnight chickpea soaking. This pass makes the timer honest and removes halal as irrelevant.
recommended_changes: Soak dried chickpeas overnight.
Cook chickpeas in garlic-cumin broth.
Serve over stale bread with harissa, olive oil, lemon, capers, tuna, or egg as options.
recipes.official_source_status: Tunisian sources consistently describe lablabi as chickpea broth with garlic and cumin poured over stale bread, finished with harissa, olive oil, lemon, capers, and optional egg or tuna.

recipe.description: Tunisian lablabi, garlic-cumin chickpea broth ladled over torn bread with harissa, olive oil, lemon, capers, and optional egg or tuna.
recipe.story: The bowl is assembled, not just served. Bread first, hot chickpeas next, then each person tunes the heat, oil, and lemon.
recipe.best_served_with: Harissa, lemon, capers, tuna, soft egg, olives, or extra olive oil

recipes.review_summary: Ojja was accurate but diet tags over-promised. This pass removes halal and gluten-free, and keeps merguez, harissa, tomato, peppers, caraway, coriander, and eggs.
recommended_changes: Brown merguez first.
Build a tomato-pepper-harissa sauce with garlic and spices.
Poach eggs gently in the sauce.
recipes.official_source_status: Tunisian sources consistently describe ojja as a tomato, pepper, harissa, garlic, and egg skillet, often with merguez or seafood.

sources.title: Le Creuset - Tunisian Ojja with Merguez

recipe.description: Tunisian ojja with merguez, eggs poached in a fiery tomato, pepper, harissa, garlic, caraway, and coriander sauce.
recipe.story: Ojja is bolder than a gentle egg breakfast. Let the harissa bloom in oil before the eggs go in.
recipe.best_served_with: Crusty bread, olives, salad, or extra harissa

recipes.review_summary: Luwombo was accurate but diet tags over-promised. This pass removes halal and keeps banana-leaf steaming with chicken and groundnut sauce.
recommended_changes: Soften banana leaves before wrapping.
Wrap chicken, vegetables, and groundnut sauce tightly.
Steam long enough for the sauce to thicken inside the parcel.
recipes.official_source_status: Ugandan references consistently describe luwombo as chicken, beef, mushrooms, fish, or groundnut sauce wrapped in banana leaves and steamed.

recipe.description: Ugandan luwombo, chicken, beef, fish, mushrooms, or groundnut sauce wrapped in banana leaves and steamed until tender.
recipe.story: The banana leaf is the pot. Seal it well and let the steam build a sauce inside the parcel.
recipe.best_served_with: Matoke, rice, posho, sweet potatoes, or greens

recipes.review_summary: Matoke was accurate. This pass removes halal as irrelevant and keeps the steamed banana-leaf method with savory tomato-onion sauce.
recommended_changes: Use green cooking bananas.
Steam wrapped in banana leaves if available.
Mash with tomato-onion sauce and serve with groundnut sauce, beans, or meat.
recipes.official_source_status: Ugandan references consistently describe matoke as green cooking bananas steamed, mashed, and served with sauce, beans, groundnut sauce, or meat.

recipe.description: Ugandan matoke, steamed green cooking bananas mashed into tomato, onion, garlic, and pepper sauce.
recipe.story: The bananas should steam until fully soft before mashing. That is how matoke becomes smooth instead of chunky.
recipe.best_served_with: Groundnut sauce, beans, beef stew, chicken luwombo, greens, or avocado

recipes.review_summary: Rolex was accurate. This pass removes halal, keeps chapati plus vegetable omelette, and adds a slightly longer chapati rest.
recommended_changes: Cook chapati first.
Make a thin vegetable omelette.
Roll the two together tightly while hot.
recipes.official_source_status: Ugandan references consistently describe rolex as a chapati rolled around eggs and vegetables, sold as street food.

recipe.description: Ugandan rolex, a hot chapati rolled around an egg omelette with onion, tomato, cabbage, and pepper.
recipe.story: The roll has to happen while both chapati and egg are hot, so the layers bend together instead of cracking.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, kachumbari, avocado, hot sauce, or extra cabbage
